title: Compilers vs Interpreters
subtitle: How Computers Understand Your Code!
overview: Ever wondered how a computer actually understands the code you write? In this session, students explore the journey from human-readable code to machine instructions — learning the difference between compilers and interpreters, why some languages use one over the other, and what really happens behind the scenes when you press RUN.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — diagrams of compile vs interpret flow, whiteboard sketches, activity shots.

gallery.caption: JIT — Just-In-Time compilation: the best of both worlds!

keyTopics: Source Code — The human-readable code you write (Python, C++, Java)
Machine Code — Binary instructions (0s and 1s) that the CPU actually executes
Compiler — Translates the ENTIRE source code into machine code BEFORE running
Interpreter — Translates and executes source code ONE LINE AT A TIME while running
Compiled Languages — C, C++, Rust, Go: fast execution, must compile before running
Interpreted Languages — Python, JavaScript, Ruby: flexible, runs directly, easier to debug
Compilation Steps — Lexing → Parsing → Semantic Analysis → Optimization → Code Generation
JIT (Just-In-Time) Compilation — Hybrid approach: compiles code at runtime (used in Java, modern JS engines)
Executable File — The .exe or binary output produced by a compiler
Runtime Error vs Compile-Time Error — Compiler catches errors before running; interpreter finds them as it goes
Bytecode — An intermediate step used by Java and Python (compiled to bytecode, then interpreted by a VM)
Virtual Machine (VM) — Software that runs bytecode: JVM for Java, CPython for Python

activity.title: Human Compiler vs Human Interpreter Role Play
parts: Printed code instruction sheets (simple pseudocode programs)
steps: Round 1 — Human Interpreter: Students receive a 10-line pseudocode program and execute it ONE LINE AT A TIME, writing output as they go
Round 2 — Human Compiler: Students READ the entire program first, check for errors, then produce the full output in one go
Discuss: Which felt faster? Which caught errors earlier?
Demo: Open Compiler Explorer (godbolt.org) — type a simple C function and watch the live machine code output
Demo: Run the same logic in Python — observe how it runs line by line (add a deliberate error on line 5 and see where it stops)
Compare: The C code needed compiling first; Python ran immediately but crashed mid-way
Challenge: Write a simple addition program in both Python and pseudocode-C — trace through each execution model

options: The binary 0s and 1s that a CPU runs
The human-readable code written by a programmer
The output printed by a program
A special type of compiler
quiz.explanation: Source code is what YOU write — readable text in Python, C++, Java, etc. The computer cannot run it directly; it must first be translated into machine code.

options: Runs your code one line at a time
Translates the entire source code into machine code before the program runs
Finds and fixes bugs automatically
Downloads libraries from the internet
quiz.explanation: A compiler reads ALL your code first, translates the whole thing into machine code, and produces an executable file. Only then does the program run — like translating a whole book before reading it!

options: Compiles all code into a .exe file
Translates and executes code one line at a time while the program is running
Checks only for syntax errors
Converts machine code back into source code
quiz.explanation: An interpreter reads one line, executes it, reads the next line, executes it — like a live translator reading aloud sentence by sentence. Python works this way!

quiz.q: Which of the following is a COMPILED language?
options: Python
JavaScript
Ruby
quiz.explanation: C++ is a compiled language! You write code → run the compiler → get a fast .exe file. Python, JavaScript, and Ruby are interpreted languages.

quiz.q: Which of the following is an INTERPRETED language?
options: C
Rust
Python
Go
quiz.explanation: Python is interpreted! When you press Run, Python reads and executes your code line by line without compiling it into a separate file first.

options: Python code written very fast
The programming language used by robots
Binary instructions (0s and 1s) that the CPU directly understands and executes
Code written inside a virtual machine
quiz.explanation: Machine code is the lowest level — pure binary (0s and 1s) that the CPU hardware can execute directly. All high-level code must eventually become machine code!

quiz.q: A compiled program finds most errors:
options: While the user is typing
Only after the program finishes running
Before the program runs, during the compilation step
Never — compiled programs have no errors
quiz.explanation: Compilers check the ENTIRE code before running. If there's an error on line 200, you'll know before running a single line — saving time and preventing runtime surprises!

quiz.q: An interpreted program hits an error on line 8. What happens to lines 1–7?
options: They are all skipped
They have already been executed successfully
They are re-run after the error is fixed
They are compiled into machine code
quiz.explanation: The interpreter already ran lines 1–7 before reaching the error on line 8. This is a key difference from a compiler, which would catch the error before running ANY line.

options: A Python script file
An executable file produced by a compiler containing machine code
A file that stores error messages
A type of virtual machine
quiz.explanation: A .exe (executable) is the OUTPUT of a compiler! It contains machine code ready for the CPU to run directly — no source code or compiler needed at runtime.

options: Compiling code exactly at midnight
A hybrid approach that compiles code at runtime for faster execution
A way to interpret code backwards
A method to compress source code
quiz.explanation: JIT compiles code WHILE it is running — getting the speed of compiled code with the flexibility of interpretation. Java and modern JavaScript engines (like V8) use JIT!

quiz.q: Java uses a special intermediate step. After compiling, Java produces:
options: A .exe file for direct CPU execution
Bytecode that runs on the Java Virtual Machine (JVM)
Pure Python code
Assembly language
quiz.explanation: Java compiles to BYTECODE — a middle ground between source code and machine code. The JVM (Java Virtual Machine) then interprets/JIT-compiles that bytecode. This is why Java runs on any device!

quiz.q: Which best describes the SPEED difference between compiled and interpreted programs?
options: Interpreted programs are always faster
Compiled programs generally run faster because translation is done before execution
They always run at exactly the same speed
Speed depends only on the computer's RAM
quiz.explanation: Compiled programs are generally faster at runtime — the hard translation work is already done! Interpreted programs translate on-the-fly, adding overhead. That's why C++ games run faster than Python scripts.

quiz.q: Which is easier to DEBUG and test quickly — compiled or interpreted?
options: Compiled — because it checks everything upfront
Interpreted — because you can run code immediately without a compile step
They are equally easy to debug
Neither can be debugged
quiz.explanation: Interpreted languages like Python are faster to test! No compile step means you can write one line and run it instantly. Great for learning, prototyping, and quick experiments.

quiz.q: What is a Virtual Machine (VM) in the context of programming languages?
options: A physical computer inside another computer
Software that executes bytecode, making programs portable across different hardware
A virus protection tool
A compiler for machine code
quiz.explanation: A Virtual Machine (like JVM or CPython) is software that runs bytecode. Because the VM handles hardware differences, the same bytecode runs on Windows, Mac, and Linux — write once, run anywhere!

quiz.q: You write a program in C++ with an error on line 50. You try to run it. What happens FIRST?
options: Lines 1–49 execute, then the program crashes at line 50
The compiler reads all 50 lines, finds the error, and shows a compile-time error before running anything
The program runs and the error is silently ignored
The interpreter runs the program line by line until line 50
quiz.explanation: C++ is compiled! The compiler scans ALL the code first. It finds the error on line 50 and refuses to produce an executable — nothing runs until the code is error-free.
